title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Secrets of the Deep: The Rise of Submerged Archaeology
content.text: For decades, archaeology was a discipline associated with dusty deserts and brush-wielding scholars in sun-scorched trenches. However, a silent revolution has been taking place beneath the waves. Submerged archaeology, once the domain of treasure hunters and salvage divers, has evolved into a sophisticated scientific field capable of rewriting human history. || (41) || [41] This transition from 'looting' to 'learning' has been fueled by technological leaps [/41]. High-resolution sonar and remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) now allow researchers to map entire sunken cities with millimetric precision. These tools have opened up environments that were previously too deep or too dangerous for human divers to explore. || (42) || [42] One such site is the legendary Pavlopetri, off the coast of Greece [/42]. It is arguably the oldest submerged town in the world, dating back over 5,000 years. Unlike shipwrecks, which represent a single moment in time, Pavlopetri offers a glimpse into a functioning Bronze Age society, with intact house plans, courtyards, and even specialized workshops. || (43) || [43] However, the logistical hurdles of working in such an environment remain immense [/43]. Every minute spent underwater is a battle against the clock and the physical limitations of the human body. Divers must deal with decompression sickness, limited visibility, and the corrosive nature of saltwater, which can destroy organic materials the moment they are exposed to air. || (44) || [44] To counter this, many teams are now turning to 'in-situ' conservation [/40]. Rather than raising every artifact to the surface—a process that is both expensive and risky—archaeologists are developing ways to preserve sites exactly where they lie. This might involve covering ruins with specialized fabrics or using sandbags to prevent erosion from powerful undercurrents. || (45) || [45] But technology is only half the battle; the legal status of these sites is equally murky [/45]. International waters are often a 'no-man's land' where site protection is difficult to enforce. While the UNESCO Convention on the Protection of the Underwater Cultural Heritage exists, many nations have yet to ratify it, leaving thousands of shipwrecks vulnerable to commercial exploitation. || (46) || [46] Despite these challenges, the rewards of underwater exploration are incomparable [/46]. As sea levels continue to rise due to climate change, the techniques perfected by today’s maritime archaeologists may soon be needed to document and protect our own coastal heritage. The deep sea is not just a graveyard of the past; it is a mirror reflecting our uncertain future.

paragraphs.A: Beyond the physical dangers, there is the staggering cost of excavation. Renting research vessels and maintaining high-tech equipment can run into tens of thousands of dollars per day. This financial pressure often forces archaeologists to make difficult choices about which parts of a site to prioritize.
paragraphs.B: [41] Historically, the field was plagued by individuals seeking gold rather than knowledge [/41]. It wasn't until the mid-20th century, with the invention of the aqualung, that legitimate scientists could spend enough time on the seabed to conduct systematic surveys and apply the same rigors as their land-based colleagues.
paragraphs.C: [46] This is because the cold, anaerobic conditions of the deep ocean can preserve materials like wood, leather, and textiles for centuries [/46]. On land, such items would have rotted away long ago, but in the deep, they remain as 'time capsules' providing a level of detail that land-based sites can rarely match.
paragraphs.D: [42] These advancements have led to discoveries that have stunned the academic community [/42]. Entire landscapes that were flooded at the end of the last Ice Age are now being surveyed, revealing how early humans adapted to rapidly changing climates and rising sea levels.
paragraphs.E: [45] Commercial treasure hunters often argue that they are performing a public service by bringing artifacts to light [/45]. Archaeologists, however, maintain that removing items without recording their exact context destroys their historical value, turning a piece of history into a mere commodity.
paragraphs.F: [43] These urban ruins provide a unique challenge because of their sheer scale [/43]. Mapping a street layout underwater requires a level of coordination and endurance that far exceeds that of a standard archaeological dig on land.
paragraphs.G: [44] This delicate state of preservation means that traditional excavation methods can often do more harm than good [/44]. Once a wooden hull that has been waterlogged for centuries hits the oxygen-rich atmosphere, it can warp and crumble within hours if not treated with expensive chemicals.

questions.explanation: B introduces the historical context of 'gold hunters' and the invention of the aqualung, which connects perfectly with the next sentence about the 'transition from looting to learning fueled by technological leaps'.
D mentions 'advancements' and 'stunning discoveries' in general, which the main text then specifies with 'One such site is the legendary Pavlopetri'.

The main text describes Pavlopetri as a town with house plans. F refers to these as 'urban ruins' and mentions the challenge of their scale, leading into the text's next point about 'logistical hurdles'.
The main text mentions the corrosive nature of saltwater and air exposure. G explains the 'delicate state of preservation' (wood warping), which leads the main text to suggest 'in-situ' conservation.
E discusses the conflict between treasure hunters and archaeologists, which fits the main text's mention of the 'murky legal status' and the vulnerability to 'commercial exploitation'.
C explains why the rewards are 'incomparable' by highlighting the unique preservation of organic materials (wood, leather) in deep-sea conditions.
